s1_b1: A lit match. And a warm bathtub.
s1_b2: The flame is far hotter. Hundreds of degrees against a mild forty.
s1_b3: Now: which one could you safely touch — and which one could heat a whole room?
s1_b4: The match is hotter, but it can't warm the room. The cooler tub can. Hotter isn't the same as more heat.
s1_b5: Zoom in. Heat is really the jiggling of countless particles. Always moving, always bumping.
s1_b6: Temperature measures how violently each particle jiggles, on average. Just the average. Per particle.
s1_b7: In the match flame, each particle jiggles like mad. Very high temperature.
s1_b8: But there are almost none of them. A tiny flame holds very little jiggling in total.
s1_b9: The tub is only mildly warm — but it's a vast crowd of particles, all jiggling a little.
s1_b10: Add up all that jiggling and the tub holds an ocean of energy. That total is thermal energy.
s1_b11: Temperature is the per-particle average. Thermal energy is the grand total. Intensity versus amount.
s1_b12: Average jiggle versus the whole sum — and how many particles bridges them. Quantifying that link is yours.
